fullPlan.projectDescription: Replace the current in-memory job queue (Map<jobId, Job> in src/queue/memory.ts) with a Redis-backed queue supporting at-least-once delivery, dead-letter handling, and configurable retry. Migration must preserve the producer/consumer API contract so existing callers continue to compile and function without modification. Goals: durability + horizontal scale (queue survives process restart, multiple consumer instances can share a single queue).
nonGoals: Do not change the producer/consumer API — existing callers must compile without modification
Do not introduce new authentication or credentials beyond the existing REDIS_URL environment variable
Do not remove the in-memory implementation — keep behind a feature flag for rollback
Do not support exactly-once delivery — at-least-once with idempotency_key is sufficient
Do not implement priority queues, scheduled (delayed) jobs, or cron-like features in this project
Do not implement a DLQ replay or requeue API — retention is passive TTL only

phases.title: Queue interface contract + in-memory alignment
phases.description: Define the canonical QueueInterface<T> type contract and align MemoryQueue to implement it. Locks the API surface that all subsequent phases and all existing callers depend on.
acceptanceCriteria: QueueInterface<T> exported from src/queue/types.ts with enqueue, dequeue, ack, nack, and size method signatures
MemoryQueue<T> in src/queue/memory.ts implements QueueInterface<T> without changing its external behavior
src/queue/index.ts re-exports QueueInterface and MemoryQueue; existing import paths continue to resolve
TypeScript build passes with zero errors (tsc --noEmit)
Unit tests cover MemoryQueue: enqueue, dequeue, ack, nack, and empty-queue dequeue returns null

executorDecides: Generic type parameter name (T, TJob, etc.)
Whether dequeue returns T | null or Promise<T | null>
Internal MemoryQueue data structures beyond the Map constraint
Test file location (co-located vs __tests__ subdirectory)
escalateToOperator: Any method added to QueueInterface beyond enqueue/dequeue/ack/nack/size
Breaking change to existing caller import paths

phases.title: Feature flag + queue factory adapter
phases.description: Build the createQueue factory and QUEUE_BACKEND env-var routing that selects MemoryQueue or RedisQueue at startup. Provides the 5-minute rollback mechanism required by the reversibility constraint.
acceptanceCriteria: createQueue<T>(config) factory exported from src/queue/index.ts returns a QueueInterface<T>
QUEUE_BACKEND=memory routes to MemoryQueue; QUEUE_BACKEND=redis routes to RedisQueue (stub accepted in this phase)
Invalid QUEUE_BACKEND value throws at startup with a descriptive message, not at first enqueue
Changing QUEUE_BACKEND and restarting the process switches backends without any code changes
Unit tests cover factory routing for both valid backend values and the invalid-value throw path

executorDecides: Config object shape passed to createQueue (env-read inside factory vs caller-supplied object)
How to stub RedisQueue for this phase (interface-conforming throw-on-use class)
Whether QUEUE_BACKEND defaults to 'memory' or is required
escalateToOperator: Additional flag mechanism beyond QUEUE_BACKEND env var
Runtime flag switching without process restart

phases.title: Redis connection wrapper + docker-compose fixture
phases.description: Build the ioredis connection lifecycle wrapper and the docker-compose fixture used by integration tests in p7. Can proceed in parallel with p2 after p1 lands.
acceptanceCriteria: RedisConnection class wraps ioredis, exposes connect(), disconnect(), and client getter
connect() reads REDIS_URL from environment; throws a clear error if REDIS_URL is unset when QUEUE_BACKEND=redis
healthCheck() returns true when Redis is reachable, false on connection error (never throws)
docker-compose.yml starts a Redis instance on a deterministic port usable by integration tests
Unit tests cover connection lifecycle using a mock ioredis client (no real Redis required)

executorDecides: ioredis reconnect strategy and timeout values
Whether RedisConnection is a class or a factory function
docker-compose Redis image version (latest stable)
escalateToOperator: TLS or auth config beyond REDIS_URL

phases.title: RedisQueue core — enqueue and dequeue
phases.description: Implement the core Redis data model and the enqueue/dequeue operations. Dequeue atomically moves a job from the pending list to an inflight sorted set (by timestamp), establishing the foundation for at-least-once semantics in p5.
acceptanceCriteria: RedisQueue<T> implements QueueInterface<T>; ack/nack may be no-op stubs in this phase
enqueue(job) serializes job to JSON, appends to Redis list QUEUE:{name}:pending, and returns a job ID
dequeue() atomically moves the next job from the pending list to QUEUE:{name}:inflight sorted set with a timestamp score
size() returns LLEN of QUEUE:{name}:pending
Two concurrent RedisQueue instances sharing the same Redis keyspace cannot both dequeue the same job (mutual exclusion via atomic command)
Unit tests with a mock ioredis client cover enqueue, dequeue, size, and empty-queue dequeue returns null

executorDecides: Redis key naming scheme (namespace, separator characters)
Job ID generation strategy (uuid, nanoid, timestamp+random)
Lua script vs LMOVE for the atomic pending→inflight transition
JSON serialization approach for job payload
escalateToOperator: Switching the data model from list+sorted-set to Redis Streams
Storing job metadata in a separate hash rather than inline in the payload

phases.title: At-least-once delivery — ack, nack, and visibility timeout
phases.description: Complete the at-least-once delivery contract: ack removes a job from inflight, nack returns it to pending immediately, and a background daemon re-queues jobs whose consumers crashed without acking.
acceptanceCriteria: ack(jobId) removes the job from QUEUE:{name}:inflight; calling ack on an unknown ID is a no-op (not an error)
nack(jobId) moves the job from inflight back to the pending list without incrementing retry count
Visibility timeout daemon polls the inflight sorted set and re-queues any job whose score is older than visibilityTimeoutMs
visibilityTimeoutMs is configurable per RedisQueue instance, not hardcoded
Unit tests cover: ack removes job from inflight; nack returns job to pending; daemon re-queues an overdue job; daemon ignores a job still within the timeout window

executorDecides: Daemon polling interval (reasonable default, e.g. visibilityTimeoutMs / 2)
setInterval vs recursive setTimeout for the daemon
Whether nack prepends (LPUSH) or appends (RPUSH) back to the pending list
escalateToOperator: Replacing daemon polling with Redis keyspace notifications or Streams consumer groups

phases.title: Retry logic and dead-letter queue
phases.description: Wire retry counting into nack and implement DLQ promotion. Jobs that exhaust retries are moved to a DLQ key with a 14-day TTL. All retry parameters are configuration-driven.
acceptanceCriteria: nack(jobId) increments the job's retry count in Redis; if retryCount < maxRetries, re-queues with exponential backoff delay; if retryCount >= maxRetries, promotes job to QUEUE:{name}:dlq
maxRetries and backoff parameters (base delay, multiplier) are configurable per RedisQueue instance
Each DLQ entry has a Redis TTL of exactly 1209600 seconds (14 days) set at promotion time
deadLetterSize() returns the count of entries in QUEUE:{name}:dlq
Unit tests cover: first nack re-queues with base delay; subsequent nack uses longer delay; max-retry exceeded promotes to DLQ; DLQ TTL is set to 1209600 on the promoted entry

executorDecides: Where retry count is stored (hash field, inline in serialized payload, or separate key)
Backoff strategy detail (pure exponential vs exponential with jitter)
DLQ data structure (list vs sorted set keyed by failure timestamp)
escalateToOperator: DLQ replay or requeue API (explicitly out of scope)
Per-job configurable TTL beyond the 14-day minimum
Alerting or notification on DLQ promotion

phases.title: Integration test suite and latency validation
phases.description: End-to-end integration tests against a real Redis instance using the docker-compose fixture from p3, covering all delivery guarantees. Includes a p99 enqueue latency benchmark enforcing the 10ms constraint.
acceptanceCriteria: Integration test suite starts and stops the docker-compose Redis fixture automatically (globalSetup/globalTeardown or equivalent vitest mechanism)
Full enqueue→dequeue→ack cycle passes against real Redis with no mocks
nack→retry cycle test: job is re-queued N-1 times then promoted to DLQ on the Nth nack
Visibility timeout recovery test: a job left unacked is re-queued by the daemon within 2× visibilityTimeoutMs
Feature flag test: QUEUE_BACKEND=memory creates a MemoryQueue; QUEUE_BACKEND=redis creates a RedisQueue
Latency benchmark: p99 of 1000 sequential enqueue calls against the docker-compose Redis instance is ≤ 10ms (test fails and reports the actual p99 if exceeded)

executorDecides: docker-compose integration strategy (vitest globalSetup shell-out vs testcontainers library)
Latency percentile calculation (manual sort vs a stats helper)
Whether integration tests run in the same vitest suite or a separate npm script
escalateToOperator: CI pipeline changes required to provision Redis in the test environment
Latency target adjustment if the docker-compose Redis cannot meet 10ms p99 on the target hardware

fullPlan.decompositionRationale: Seven phases reflect the natural dependency layers of the migration. p1 locks the interface contract that all phases and existing callers share — nothing can start without it. p2 (feature flag adapter) and p3 (Redis connection + docker-compose) both depend only on p1 and can run in parallel; p4 needs both to land before RedisQueue can be fully wired. p4–p6 stack vertically: core data model, then delivery semantics (ack/nack/visibility), then failure path (retry/DLQ) — each phase is a coherent slice of the Redis implementation that builds directly on the previous one's data structures. p7 closes with end-to-end validation against real Redis, which requires all delivery guarantees to be in place. The critical path runs through p2 rather than p3 because p4 depends on the adapter to slot RedisQueue in; p3 proceeds in parallel. Phase count of 7 sits comfortably in the 6–12 target: p1/p2/p3 are small (types and wiring), p4–p7 are medium (non-trivial Redis ops, integration harness, benchmark). Splitting p5 (consumer-side contract) from p6 (failure-path contract) avoids a single unwieldy phase covering all delivery guarantees while keeping each phase's scope coherent and independently merge-ready.
